"Christopher Pomasl" <pomasl-NOSpam@starband.net> wrote in message 
news:pan.2006.01.30.04.40.18.575020@starband.net...
> On Sun, 29 Jan 2006 21:22:54 -0700, Christopher Pomasl wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> I could do a lot more with a larger team, but I get by with just myself.

Your comment reminded me of a man I once contracted for. He got very rich 
from the efforts of our team but was loath to share any of this bounty. I 
refused to do further work for him when the job finished, and we parted, not 
amicably, but civilly.

Some years later I bumped into him in a bar in London and his scathing 
greeting (in a loud voice, for the benefit of the people I was with) was: 
"Oh, Hello, Peter... still a one man band are we?"

Without hesitation I replied: "Well, actually, X, I find it impossible to 
get by with any less..."  There was general laughter all round and he beat a 
hasty retreat. I've never seen him since and never mourned the loss.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol code assessment

- **From:** "Grzegorz Mazur" <news@gregu.ihateunwantedmail.cjb.net>
- **Date:** 2006-02-01T20:32:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<drr5v0.3cg.1@hamster.gregu.cjb.net>`
- **References:** `<1138416722.370897.6450@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
<apple.time@yahoo.com wrote:
> My boss just asked me to do a Cobol code assessment for our company.
> He has given me no guidelines on what is expected of me.  I have worked
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> information, of course!)  THANKS

I hope this will help you slightly. I am now working on a project in a major 
bank, programming in Cobol like hell. The whole system we are implementing 
is written in Cobol and since the amount of people involved is great, the 
system was initially written in mid-90's in another country, etc., several 
rules have been established. Another problem is that many (like myself for 
example) are beginners to Cobol / mainframe programming, so extra checks are 
useful.

The set of rules is extensive, but they contain a lot of issues related to 
both SQL code and Cobol code itself. While I cannot remember them all, here 
are some examples of critical rules (i.e. you cannot promote your code above 
development tests if they are not followed):

1). In cursors, ALWAYS state "FOR FETCH ONLY" or "FOR UPDATE OF".
2). NEVER do "SELECT * ...."
3). When doing "SELECT ..... INTO", ALWAYS specify the separate variables, 
not just the copybook.
4). Use "WITH UR" whenever possible. In other cases use "WITH CS". Only in 
critical cases go higher than that (i.e. rule can be relaxed on an 
individual basis).

(they are all SQL-related, since I work with queries a lot and remember them 
better than others)

Some other issues could be:

1). Multiple "MOVE xxx TO yyy" lines should be aligned so that "TO" is in 
one column, for code readability. (this one is pretty weak in terms of 
evaluation of the code, I think)
2). All "IF"s, "PERFORM UNTIL"s, "EVALUATE"s MUST have a proper "END-" bit.
3). You should not use "DISPLAY" in batch programs. (we have a special 
routine to write to sysout from batch programs, thus the rule)

You can easily see the logic here - if there is "ALWAYS", "MUST" or "CANNOT" 
in the rule, this is critical and will cause the code to be non-promotable. 
All other will generate warnings and your code will promote fine, but your 
ego will be impacted ;)

The technical team gathers statistics periodically for all teams involved. 
They also penalize not keeping to naming rules (not specified above, since 
they are rather system/situation specific) etc.

If you want, I can ask whether this information is in any way confidential 
to my company and perhaps I can share the list of errors produced by the 
verification script. While the usability of this information for you might 
be minimal, it still may help.

Good luck :)

Greg.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol code assessment

- **From:** "Grzegorz Mazur" <news@gregu.ihateunwantedmail.cjb.net>
- **Date:** 2006-02-02T20:19:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<drtph2.20o.1@hamster.gregu.cjb.net>`
- **References:** `<1138416722.370897.6450@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <drr5v0.3cg.1@hamster.gregu.cjb.net> <ei32u15t37rvul3mrq5lajqmnjndcp8cii@4ax.com>`

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Wed, 1 Feb 2006 20:32:34 +0100, "Grzegorz Mazur"
> <news@gregu.ihateunwantedmail.cjb.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> so that DISPLAY works as desired.

That's why the special comment is there :)

Actually most of these rules are implementation specific, i.e. on small 
dedicated systems a full table scan could be no problem, but in banking you 
tend to avoid situations, where you scan a table with half a billion rows...

Greg.
```

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-02-02T01:21:59+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com>`

```

"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
news:droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com...
>
> In article <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm dubious of the prospects of writing bug-free nontrivial components.

So am I; that's NOT what I'm advocating... :-)

My experience over the last 5 or 6 years is that the simpler the components 
are, the more reuse they get. 'Nontrivial' functionality is comprised of 
smaller, decomposed, functionality. It's like building with Lego. You don't 
change the bricks.

>
>> The rule of source code is over. The future belongs to object code.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and their connections.

Well, that's an interesting argument. Would you say that 'scripting' and 
maintaining script, is the same as 'programming'? It is certainly arguable. 
But I think you would agree that  the script required to glue a bunch of 
components into an application has nothing like the volume, verbosity or 
complexity of the all the lines making up those components, irrespective of 
the language(s) they're written in. I posted an example here a long time ago 
(some years back) of a scripted application that required something like 60 
lines to do what would have taken several thousand lines of procedural code. 
There is no comparison.
>
> When I was at IBM one of the products I worked on was Data Explorer,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have seen a similar product form MicroSoft that was used for building RDB 
procedures  and triggers. Most impressive. Finished, working, bug free, 
application in minutes from a graphic IDE without a single line of code 
(script or procedural) in sight. Just drag and drop components. I believe 
that is the future.

> What we saw during testing was that users would start off with simple
> graphs, then elaborate them to add new function, refactor common tasks
> into small graphs that they would package as macros, and so forth.
> They were writing source code - it was just source code where each
> "statement" was in fact a component.

Users who use graphs are likely to do that.
>
> I believe components will continue to be important in software
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I agree.

> However, I think the component "revolution" has been oversold.  To my
> mind, it's nothing more than the continuing evolution of programming
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There we have to differ. I believe it does. It has certainly changed the way 
I develop systems. Radically.

I don't agree about it being oversold; if anything, it is undersold. Just as 
OO was undersold to traditional programmers.

I embraced OO because I saw it as enabling a true reusable, transportable, 
encapsulated, component based solution. And I believe components are the 
Keys to the Kingdom. I arrived at this opinion based almost entirely on my 
own experience because none of the places I worked even implemented OO, 
never mind components.  Very few wanted to talk about ActiveX or COM or even 
Beans and CORBA.

From the day I first got remote procedure calls working in a DCOM+ 
environment that 'everyone knew' was unsafe and unstable and vulnerabe to 
attack and could never work, and I had to be crazy to even think of 
developing in it, I was sold.
(No-one has ever successfully hacked it. There are safeguards in place, both 
hardware and software. Of course there is always a risk, but all risks have 
mitigations.)

Suddenly my applications could be distributed around the network, could load 
level themselves, and could reach across vast distances (this app. ran in 
Australia... it's a big place) just as if they were running on a standalone 
desktop. One copy of encapsulated functionality, running anywhere you want 
it. I was sold then and I'm sold now.

Today I see millions of dollars being spent (I just finished costing my 
project :-)) on OO Java Bean component based software that is easy to 
maintain (extend it with some more Lego blocks), flexible, responsive and 
ready for the future, not stuck in the rule of maintainable source code that 
has governed this industry since the 1960s.

I see bright young people enthusiastically working together in Joint 
Application Development workshops that bring the functionality needed right 
now to our business; people who are shooting the Rapids, not going over the 
Waterfall. Goal oriented, timeboxed, RAD development.  The atmosphere is 
energetic and excited. I have to make people go home... :-)

It is a far cry from the days of sitting at a green screen writing COBOL 
with SPF. (I enjoyed that too, and I wouldn't knock it because it fed me for 
many years, but I wouldn't go back to it now...)

 >> Maintining source code is yesterday's technology.
>
> Component authors will still have to maintain the source to those
> components.  I don't see anything obviating that need.
Again that's where we differ. If you maintain a component, you shouldn't 
have written it as a component. I have components that I wrote years ago, 
they are successfully deployed in live appplications and I have NEVER 
TOUCHED THEM since they were written. The only time I would consider 
modifying component code is while it is being tested and debugged. Once it 
does what it is specified to do, its methods, properties, and events are 
documented and that's what it does. Nothing more, nothing less. If you want 
to modify the way a certain method works you wrap the component and either 
override the method.via an interface, or write a new method in the wrapper. 
Encapsulated functionality is exactly that...encapsulated.

Most people used to traditional environments have trouble with this and I 
understand why they do. Again, I reached this conclusion based on my own 
experience and finding what works for me.

And people
> building applications from components will discover that they need to
> maintain their component arrangements - which is just source
> maintenance in a different guise.
>
Yes, it is certainly useful to experiment with different arrangements and 
that's where a GUI IDE comes in handy. This is the same argument as above; 
is script actually source? I don't think so, but I would agree it is 
arguable. I predicate my argument on the fact that glue is not considered to 
be timber, and, so far as I know, no one has ever built a house (or anything 
else) from glue. The material used to stick a construction together is not 
the same as the construction itself, although it is certainly part of it. 
You can hardly call it 'source maintenance' if you have machinery that 
allows you to experiment with different positions and combinations of 
building blocks, and then, when you are satisfied with the final 
arrangement, injects the necessary glue for you at the right places...

>> But I also think people need to start thinking 'beyond the square'. 
>> Todays
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> left the mainstream.

OK, I grant that. There are still people who make flint tools and speak 
Latin. Most of them do it for fun or because their profession requires them 
to investigate and understand these things. They are, as you say, 
'specialized practitioners'.  Could we exclude these from  the argument, 
having agreed this point? My statement was referring to the world at large.


> But speculating about this industry fifty years
> from now, while entertaining, isn't likely to be very convincing; we
> don't have enough history to meaningfully extrapolate for a quarter
> of that time.

12 and a half years from now? Sure...step right up and meet August, 2018...

1. Wireless internet will be the norm and will pervade our lives. Most 
people will carry a device or accessory that connects them to it instantly. 
'Intelligent jewellery' will be a sought after accessory; brooches, 
cufflinks, spectacles that all talk to you using their internet connection 
to keep you informed instantly..

2. Roads will be tolled, cars will  be made of non-metallic substances, run 
on hydrogen (or water) and everyone in China will want one. (they will be 
allowed one small car per family, just as they are allowed one small child 
per family...)

3. Gorillas will be extinct in the wild.

4. Large scale supersonic air transport will be available. London to Los 
Angeles in 4 hours.

5. The 2002 COBOL standard will be implemented by a consortium comprised of 
the remnants of MicroFocus, AcuCOBOL, and Bendix (all the other vendors will 
have moved away from it long before. Bendix decided there was no money in 
washing machines because everybody has one, and COBOL sounds like an 
interesting place...wasn't Adama in Battlestar Galactica one of the Lords of 
COBOL...? Anyway, might as well spend what's left of the company on 
something that might be fun...

See, this pyschic stuff is easy... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-02-01T07:24:32-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<rqg1u1d2pbuk23ufvlq91manc0blfrqfl6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net>`

```
In my shop, we have JBuilder which apparently has the ability to drag
and drop GUI components.   But we don't use Java for that.

Java is used between the data and the web pages.   XML does the GUI
stuff.

The direction I see us continuing to move in is having databases and
portals as our core elements.

The technology in our databases and warehouses is pretty stock.   The
technology on the web browsers is pretty stock.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-02-06T15:24:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net>`

```

In article <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes:
> "Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
> news:droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> change the bricks.

That's fine in theory, and no doubt often achievable in practice.
However, there are simply going to be cases where a component can't
be decomposed into definitely-bug-free pieces, and cases where it
has to change because of a change in requirements that can't be
captured by specialization (at least not in a sensible fashion).

For an example of the former, consider a GUI component that renders
an image map.  Image rendering is notorious for security holes; we've
recently gone through another spate of these vulnerabilities.  Any
component that renders an image is potentially vulnerable to issues
such as integer overflows, and that's not something that can be
usefully decomposed into trivial components.  There's no reasonable
option other than to put the (necessarily complex) processing of the
image data format into a component and maintain it.

For the latter, consider a calendar component which has rules for
daylight savings time.  Either those rules can come from processing
DST rules files - in which case you have much the same problem that
you have with image files above, plus questions of how those rules
files are located, etc - or they're hard-coded, in which case there
are localization issues.  Moreover, you have situations like what
we're faced with in the US right now, with a pending change to the
rules for all the regions which observe DST, plus an incipient switch
in most of Indiana to observing DST - though the timezone differs in
different parts of the state, and a significant portion of the
population appears determined to ignore it.  That's a change in
requirements which can't be anticipated by a trivial component.

> >> The rule of source code is over. The future belongs to object code.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintaining script, is the same as 'programming'? It is certainly arguable. 

Yes, I would.  Scripting languages are programming languages; I don't
even see a clear distinction.  "Scripting" seems to be an informal
description applied to some high-level languages with primitives for
executing external commands.  Aside from that, I don't know what makes
Perl, for example, a different kind of language from, say, C.  They're
both Turing-complete, compiled, general-purpose languages that have
been used for everything from trivial one-off utilities to large
production systems.

> But I think you would agree that  the script required to glue a bunch of 
> components into an application has nothing like the volume, verbosity or 
> complexity of the all the lines making up those components, irrespective of 
> the language(s) they're written in.

That's generally true, yes, though I'd be reluctant to make a maxim
of it.  Judicious use of highly-expressive languages can get a great
deal done in a few lines of code (for an exercise in the OCaml book
I'm reading, I just wrote an entire dictionary system, suitable for
eg a spell-checker, in under 50 lines of source), and I've seen
component-based systems that used an awful lot of components and glue
code.

> I have seen a similar product form MicroSoft that was used for building RDB 
> procedures  and triggers. Most impressive. Finished, working, bug free, 
> application in minutes from a graphic IDE without a single line of code 
> (script or procedural) in sight. Just drag and drop components. I believe 
> that is the future.

The future for accomplishing certain tasks, perhaps; I think it's a
good goal to aim for in much application development.  But I don't
see anyone building, say, a production DBMS this way, or a real-time
3D rendering engine.

> > However, I think the component "revolution" has been oversold.  To my
> > mind, it's nothing more than the continuing evolution of programming
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I develop systems. Radically.

Is it more radical than the shift from plug-board wiring to stored-
program computers?  From hand-assembling to software assemblers?  From
assemblers to early high-level languages like COBOL?  To structured
programming?  From programming the "bare metal" to running under a
monitor, and then a full-blown OS?

I don't think so.  I think those (and several others) were all
advances of approximately the same order.  For individual practi-
tioners, yes, they often marked radical changes in the way they
personally created software, but over the course of the (short)
history of the industry, they represent a steady, if steep,
evolution.

> I don't agree about it being oversold; if anything, it is undersold. Just as 
> OO was undersold to traditional programmers.

I'll agree it's under-adopted.  But I think billing it as revolution-
ary actually is partly to blame for that.  We work in an industry
where there's far too much hype, and consequently too much noise and
a concommittant cynicism.

> From the day I first got remote procedure calls working in a DCOM+ 
> environment that 'everyone knew' was unsafe and unstable and vulnerabe to 
> attack and could never work, and I had to be crazy to even think of 
> developing in it, I was sold.

Well, DCOM+ *is* vulnerable to attack.  It has been attacked, often
and successfully.  That's simple fact.

> (No-one has ever successfully hacked it. There are safeguards in place, both 
> hardware and software. Of course there is always a risk, but all risks have 
> mitigations.)

Unfortunately, in security, negative anecdotes don't demonstrate
anything useful; there are plenty of insecure systems that have never
had a breach detected.  That's no comfort to the people who own
systems that *were* compromised (all of which, of course, had never
been "hacked" right up until the point when they first were).

However, DCOM+ is beside the point.  The quality of a particular
component implementation (distributed or not) doesn't say anything
about the usefulness of a component architecture in general.

> Suddenly my applications could be distributed around the network, could load 
> level themselves, and could reach across vast distances (this app. ran in 
> Australia... it's a big place) just as if they were running on a standalone 
> desktop. One copy of encapsulated functionality, running anywhere you want 
> it. I was sold then and I'm sold now.

Sure.  Distributed applications have been paying my salary since
1991, and even in 1988 I was working on a commercial multi-tier,
multi-protocol middleware product.

I agree that most front-end applications can be written by gluing
components, and that there are significant advantages to doing so;
and in many cases, at least some of those components will be
distributed (though hopefully with far more attention to security
than there has been in the past).

> >> Maintining source code is yesterday's technology.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> modifying component code is while it is being tested and debugged.

How do you know when it's free of bugs?

> Once it 
> does what it is specified to do, its methods, properties, and events are 
> documented and that's what it does. Nothing more, nothing less.

Oh, interface stability is a fine thing, and well-written components
will enforce a degree of programming by contract, which certainly
eliminates huge classes of errors.  No disagreement there.

> If you want 
> to modify the way a certain method works you wrap the component and either 
> override the method.via an interface, or write a new method in the wrapper. 
> Encapsulated functionality is exactly that...encapsulated.

I'm not advocating changing the interface.  Published interfaces
should be stable (extensions aside).  Microsoft's failure to learn
this is responsible for "DLL Hell", which in turn has created a
counterproductive move away from the most basic form of component-
ization, shared code modules.

> And people
> > building applications from components will discover that they need to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> else) from glue.

I think that's a false analogy.  Glue isn't structural; it's of a
different order.  Scripting languages have the same capabilities as
the components they assemble.  (I've never seen a "scripting
language" - at least nothing to which I'd apply the term - that isn't
Turing-complete.)

> You can hardly call it 'source maintenance' if you have machinery that 
> allows you to experiment with different positions and combinations of 
> building blocks, and then, when you are satisfied with the final 
> arrangement, injects the necessary glue for you at the right places...

I don't see why not.  I have compilers and editors that let me
experiment with different arrangements of the statements permitted in
my programming languages; how is that not such "machinery"?

When I experiment in OCaml, I use the compiler's top-level
interactive loop, which incrementally compiles each statement into
the workspace as I enter it.  Similar facilities are commonly used
for LISP, APL, and even many traditional BASICs.  How is that
qualitatively different?  (I could create a GUI representation of it
if I liked.)

> > I'm not so sure about that, either.  Many old technologies have a way
> > of remaining relevant to specialized practitioners even after they've
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> having agreed this point? My statement was referring to the world at large.

The specialized practitioners I was thinking of were more on the
lines of the programmers of embedded systems - which represent the
vast majority of computers in use, incidentally.  Many of those
systems are hard real-time, using the minimum-spec hardware required
to do the job (because in such systems every penny counts).  I don't
see them using GUI component-assembly tools, by and large, anytime
soon; as embedded hardware gets more capable, more will be demanded
of it, so there's always room in the market for the lowest-end
implementations.

> > But speculating about this industry fifty years
> > from now, while entertaining, isn't likely to be very convincing; we
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to keep you informed instantly..

I assume "most people" here refers only to the industrialized
middle-class; I think hoping for pervasive WiFi in Bangladesh in
the next decade is probably a little optimistic.

(Personally, I loathe the idea of being surrounded by people who
are constantly interacting with unseen presences, which is why I
don't live in an institution for the insane; but apparently some
folks think it'd be swell.)

> 2. Roads will be tolled, cars will  be made of non-metallic substances, run 
> on hydrogen (or water) and everyone in China will want one.

Point of order: this has nothing to do with "this industry".  I
didn't say anything about the possibility of accurately speculating
on the future of other industries.  (That said, I'm not sure I find
this terribly convincing either.  I don't see any engineering
purpose behind making cars entirely out of non-metals, and water
doesn't have much energy density.)

> 3. Gorillas will be extinct in the wild.

And who knows how many other species...

> 4. Large scale supersonic air transport will be available. London to Los 
> Angeles in 4 hours.

I don't think there's a market for it, frankly.  The Concorde was
never commercially successful.  And conventional SST over populated
landmass is unlikely to gain government approval, while HST is too
hard on the passengers.  (Even astronauts aren't fond of riding on
the "vomit comet".)

> 5. The 2002 COBOL standard will be implemented by a consortium comprised of 
> the remnants of MicroFocus, AcuCOBOL, and Bendix

Well, at least it's making better progress than the 1999 C standard.


To sum up, though, I think we're largely in agreement that component
architectures are a Good Thing; our disagreement seems to be in
quantity, not quality.  And I wouldn't be dismayed if components did
storm the battlements; there's a lot to be said for rigorous
enforcement of interfaces and interoperability and loose coupling.
We could stand some better-quality software.  Components don't
guarantee that, but they provide some of the tools to make it more
convenient, and convenience is half the battle.
```

```
On 6 Feb 2006 15:24:37 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
wrote:

>I don't think there's a market for it, frankly.  The Concorde was
>never commercially successful.  And conventional SST over populated
>landmass is unlikely to gain government approval, while HST is too
>hard on the passengers.  (Even astronauts aren't fond of riding on
>the "vomit comet".)

Having ridden on the vomit comet, (and in supersonic fighter jets) I
don't see its relationship to SST.   

The SST is less comfortable than a 747 is, but the 747 is less
comfortable than an ocean liner is.   There were wealthy people who
very much preferred the SST - but it was too expensive for most
people.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-02-07T16:50:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsaj4d01r39@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com> <8jseu1d5rbf5olq10pngat129aajhatgl3@4ax.com>`

```

In article <8jseu1d5rbf5olq10pngat129aajhatgl3@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> On 6 Feb 2006 15:24:37 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't see its relationship to SST.   

I mentioned it in relation to HST (hypersonic transport).  The HST
proposals (for commercial use) that I usually see are all parabolic -
they aren't proposing level flight.  However, I note Google found
some commercial HST proposals for transoceanic flight that appear to
use level flight.

> The SST is less comfortable than a 747 is, but the 747 is less
> comfortable than an ocean liner is.   There were wealthy people who
> very much preferred the SST - but it was too expensive for most
> people.

Too expensive to be profitable.  L/D ratios have certainly improved
since the Concorde - but no one tried to launch an improved Atlantic
SST service.  *Maybe* if someone gets a practical scramjet working
reliably enough for commercial air travel, *and* a good HST lifting
body with a decent L/D ratio (probably a waverider design), then it's
conceivable that they could keep costs sufficiently low to make it
viable for long-distance transoceanic flights.

But I don't see widespread HST over populated landmasses - too much
noise polution.  And the folks on the ground are likely to worry
about the possible effects of a crash, however unlikely it might be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-02-09T18:24:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsg1dm02gtk@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com> <8jseu1d5rbf5olq10pngat129aajhatgl3@4ax.com> <dsaj4d01r39@news3.newsguy.com> <vdnhu15dsdibho34kberkjj8fjhqg48vup@4ax.com>`

```

In article <vdnhu15dsdibho34kberkjj8fjhqg48vup@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> On 7 Feb 2006 16:50:21 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Which doesn't stop the military.   

True, but they often get away with things that the commercial
airlines wouldn't. :-)

I suspect that if an airline proposed HST flights over populated
areas of the US, they'd face considerable NIMBY opposition - a lot
more people than would be likely to purchase the service. (There
just aren't *that* many people who regularly travel coast-to-coast,
compared to the number who'd live in the flight path of, say, an
HST NYC-LA flight.)

And if they did get it into service, a daily overhead boom would
ruffle a lot more feathers than an occasional one from military
planes.  Other than people who live on or very near to a base, I
expect few people hear many supersonic booms.  When I lived in
Lincoln, Nebraska, we'd get them maybe once a week, on average,
from fighters flying out of Offutt (I assume).

But things might be different with SST/HST over the poles, or the
Australian Outback, or as I said before transoceanic flights.  If
they can get the costs down I wouldn't be surprised to see an SST
Tokyo-LA flight (or maybe Tokyo-Honolulu-LA, since the additional
speed might make up for not having a direct flight).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-02-06T17:56:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com>`

```

"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
news:ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com...
>
> there are simply going to be cases where a component [...]
> has to change because of a change in requirements that can't be
> captured by specialization (at least not in a sensible fashion).
>
[...]
>
> consider a calendar component which has rules for
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> requirements which can't be anticipated by a trivial component.

    I think that as compiler-technology progresses, this could be done via 
specialization. When a government decides to change the way DST works, they 
usually do so by announcing changes to the existing system. Your 
specialization would just be encoding the changes announced as a wrapper 
around the existing component.

    When your component starts getting bloated from several layers of 
amendments, the (JIT) optimizer could kick in and simplify the code at 
compile time or at run time.

    The big issue here is that to make sure the optimizer can do its job 
properly, the programmer has to try to make their components as free of side 
effects as possible. Outputting to console is a big one: if the old code 
outputs to console, the new optimized code will have to output the same 
thing under the same conditions. What this basically means is that code 
which outputs things to console are typically harder to optimize that code 
which doesn't, and so it becomes less desirable to extend a component in 
which there exists a codepath where something gets output to the console.

>> The only time I would consider
>> modifying component code is while it is being tested and debugged.
>
> How do you know when it's free of bugs?

    When it passes all of its unit tests? One could take the stance that if 
the component doesn't do what the client wants, but passes all the unit 
tests anyway, then that's a flaw in the design of the component, rather than 
a flaw in the code of the component itself.

    As a programmer, I spend part of my time designing interfaces and part 
of my time implementing those interfaces. With component-based programming, 
I guess I would be spending more time on the interfaces (making sure they do 
exactly what the client wants), and less time on implementation (ideally, 
never maintaining it).

    - Oliver
```

 Thanks Oliver for a supportive post. I know you were simply saying what you 
think, but I was starting to think I was the ONLY person in the world who 
could see the wisdom of this approach...:-) (It's always worrying...even if 
you're right...:-))

Few oneliners below...

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89...
>
> "Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> around the existing component.

This is EXACTLY the approach I have adopted. It works fine.
>
>    When your component starts getting bloated from several layers of 
> amendments, the (JIT) optimizer could kick in and simplify the code at 
> compile time or at run time.
>

I hadn't thought about that. It is a very good idea.

>    The big issue here is that to make sure the optimizer can do its job 
> properly, the programmer has to try to make their components as free of 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I handle this by using a property to state where the component will run 
(Client or Server). If it is on a server, the GUI messaging is overridden 
and written to the system log instead. Interactive processes (Methods) will 
not be activated if the component is on a server.

The whole presentation layer is arguable, with different people and 
organisations using different technologies. We are using Ajax and XML. The 
jury is still out as far as I'm concerned. Some of our business favour 
desktop, others favour web based; it is a sticky wicket that will require 
intensive investigation and tool selection.

Where does that leave the components? The Properties that would be set by 
interaction are preset to default, or the invoking components set them.Most 
of the time, interactive components are in the presentation layer anyway. I 
am not an evangelist of thin client and will put components anywhere that 
makes sense.  Of course, many components don't have GUI or CLI interfaces 
anyway as they are simply called by other components.

I'm currently looking at XML for the GUI for my personal stuff, and this 
means components that want to write to the Web simply write to an XML 
buffer.


>>> The only time I would consider
>>> modifying component code is while it is being tested and debugged.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Fair comment.

>    As a programmer, I spend part of my time designing interfaces and part 
> of my time implementing those interfaces. With component-based 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Exactly! :-)

Pete.

>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-02-07T17:14:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsakii01g7u@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com> <u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89>`

```

In article <u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89>, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> writes:
> "Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
> news:ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> specialization.

What can't be done by specialization?  I have a component that prints
"Hello, world", and I "specialize" it to add two integers.  If I can
add new members and methods, and override old ones, then sure, I can
implement any change I want by specialization.

The question is whether that's a *sensible* use of specialization.  I
submit that, in this case, it is not.

Consider: One of the problems with DST is the "overlapping hour"
issue.  If you have a time value in the "fall back" hour, then you
need to know whether DST had gone into effect (this was the second
instance of that hour in that day) or not (this was the first
instance) in order to correctly perform various operations on that
value, such as calculating the delta between it and other time value.

That's why C's mktime, for example, takes a DST flag; it only applies
during the "fall back" hour, but in that interval it makes a 3600-
second difference.

Now, let's consider the hypothetical component.  It has a fall-back
hour on a certain date and time.  You extend it for the new rules:
as of this date, the fall-back hour moves to *this* date and time.

Now there are two ambiguous hours in the derived component.  The
derived component needs to know whether it can trust calculations
performed by the original component, or whether it needs to adjust
them for 3600 seconds, because the original component is innocent of
the change in DST.

Well, you say, perhaps we made the code that determines whether DST
is in effect itself a virtual method, and that's what we overrode
when we specialized the component.  Good foresight - but is it
possible there are any other such aspects which need to be virtual,
but aren't?

My point is that specialization is an appropriate tool in some
circumstances, but to assume it will be appropriate in all is a
very big, and dangerous, leap.  (And performance would not be my
first concern.)

> >> The only time I would consider
> >> modifying component code is while it is being tested and debugged.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     When it passes all of its unit tests?

How do you know your unit tests have accurately tested all the
behaviors of the component in all of its possible future applications?

Unit tests typically do a poor job of covering hidden state effects,
for example.  They do a poor job of capturing broad requirements (like
resilience to malformed input).  They do a poor job of capturing
implicit requirements (particularly for security, such as resistance
to denials of service).  They do a very poor job of detecting knock-on
effects (effects on other components or systems).

An anecdote to illustrate the last, from one of Ed Nisley's columns
on embedded systems in DDJ: a vendor of home firewall appliances
decided to implement an NTP (Network Time Protocol) client, so that
his appliance would have an accurate timestamp.  So far, so good; a
correct timestamp is important for security purposes, and you don't
want to make the user have to set the time manually.  Lots of
appliances can now get the time from some source, such as TV side
channels or WVVB broadcasts, so why not your firewall?

So their engineer put together an NTP client, or used one out of a
box, and pointed it to a public NTP server at a certain US
university.  He (or she) configured it to periodically update the
time, and to retry on failure.

No doubt it passed all unit tests, because it worked precisely as
specified.

You can probably see where this is going.  Vendor sells a bunch of
these boxes.  They all sit happily in people's houses, connected to
always-on broadband connections, periodically hitting one server at
one university for the time.  More and more of them every day - this
is an off-the-shelf product sold at big box stores.  Eventually the
server gets overloaded, and some of the firewalls find their
requests timing out.  So they retry, and keep retrying until they
get an answer.

It wasn't long before these NTP queries were consuming most of the
university's entire incoming Internet bandwidth.  They had to start
blocking incoming NTP at the border to get any useful traffic in and
out - which broke all those firewall boxes, and broke anyone else
who happened to be using their NTP service.

Unit testing does not guarantee that you've completely tested a
unit; it typically leaves significant problem domains unexplored;
and it tells you very little about how a unit will behave in its
environment.

> One could take the stance that if 
> the component doesn't do what the client wants, but passes all the unit 
> tests anyway, then that's a flaw in the design of the component, rather than 
> a flaw in the code of the component itself.

I have no problem with demanding that the specification capture the
user's requirements - though as we all know, in practice this will
mean that often specifications have to be revised.  The problem is
that unit tests don't exhaustively prove compliance to specifications,
and specifications aren't the whole story anyway.

>     As a programmer, I spend part of my time designing interfaces and part 
> of my time implementing those interfaces. With component-based programming, 
> I guess I would be spending more time on the interfaces (making sure they do 
> exactly what the client wants), and less time on implementation (ideally, 
> never maintaining it).

Yes, and I agree that allowing well-specified interfaces to drive
development is a laudable goal.  Where I don't agree is that
nontrivial components can in general be ever declared finished, or
that all components can be trivial.
```

```

"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
news:dsakii01g7u@news1.newsguy.com...
>
> In article <u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89>, "Oliver Wong" 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> implement any change I want by specialization.

    You couldn't change the existing interface though, so if the client used 
the "Hello, World" component by calling a method which took zero arguments, 
you couldn't suddenly force that method to take 2 integer arguments without 
breaking something.

>
> The question is whether that's a *sensible* use of specialization.  I
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> the change in DST.

    I won't argue with you too much on this one, as the way I would 
personally implement any system which deals with time is to have a 
"timestamp" class which represents a point in time in an unambiguous way 
(e.g. milliseconds or nanoseconds since the unix epoch), and only convert 
into "human readable" format at the last minute. Display calendars, taking 
into account daylight savings, etc. to me, these are all "output" activities 
which I mentioned elsewhere is something I would avoid extending or 
specializing.

>
> Well, you say, perhaps we made the code that determines whether DST
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> but aren't?

    One of the nice things about Java is that all methods are virtual by 
default.

>
> My point is that specialization is an appropriate tool in some
> circumstances, but to assume it will be appropriate in all is a
> very big, and dangerous, leap.  (And performance would not be my
> first concern.)

Agreed.

>
>> >> The only time I would consider
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> behaviors of the component in all of its possible future applications?

    Some hardcore proponents of unit testing claim that you know this by 
definition. That is, they take the unit tests to be a sort of "requirements 
document", and as long as the unit tests pass, the requirements are 
fulfilled.

    I was sort of playing Devil's Advocate with that last post. I like the 
concept of unit testing, but I haven't been using it much in my own 
development (neither at work, nor at home).

[...]

> I agree that allowing well-specified interfaces to drive
> development is a laudable goal.  Where I don't agree is that
> nontrivial components can in general be ever declared finished, or
> that all components can be trivial.

    Yes, this is, in my opinion too, the weakest part of Pete's claims. 
However, I'm personally not so interested in whether in general components 
can actually ever be declared "finished" (for example), but more in whether 
or not component-based development is some sort of Platonic ideal we should 
strive for, or if in practice it turns out to do more harm and good (or 
something in between).

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2006-02-09T18:37:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsg26602r6f@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com> <u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89> <dsakii01g7u@news1.newsguy.com> <HqoGf.184798$AP5.144580@edtnps84>`

```

In article <HqoGf.184798$AP5.144580@edtnps84>, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> writes:
> "Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
> news:dsakii01g7u@news1.newsguy.com...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> breaking something.

This is a new method.  If I can't add new methods to the interface,
then there will be other cases where the limitations on extended
the interface prevent me from dealing with a new requirement by
specialization, and we're back to maintenance.

> > Consider: One of the problems with DST is the "overlapping hour"
> > issue.  If you have a time value in the "fall back" hour, then you
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> into "human readable" format at the last minute.

Converting *to* an ambiguous representation (such as DST) isn't the
problem; converting *from* it is.  Since there are many date data
sources which use the ambiguous representation - documents, people,
etc - you can't avoid this requirement in general.

> >> >> The only time I would consider
> >> >> modifying component code is while it is being tested and debugged.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> fulfilled.

That's merely disclaiming responsibility for unforseen interactions.
I have no sympathy for that philosophy; it's dangerous, insecure,
unprofessional, and irresponsible.  (I realize you are not personally
advocating it.)

One of the central lessons of computer security and risk management
is that systems have emergent behaviors - they're not just the sum
of their parts.

> However, I'm personally not so interested in whether in general components 
> can actually ever be declared "finished" (for example), but more in whether 
> or not component-based development is some sort of Platonic ideal we should 
> strive for, or if in practice it turns out to do more harm and good (or 
> something in between).

Well, clearly component architecture is a tool, and it can be applied
to solve or at least allieviate certain problems.  I think we're all
in agreement on that.  Strong interfaces, reuse, etc, are rarely the
Wrong Thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsi68u0283g@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com> <u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89> <dsakii01g7u@news1.newsguy.com> <HqoGf.184798$AP5.144580@edtnps84> <dsg26602r6f@news3.newsguy.com> <bWPGf.267023$OU5.121834@clgrps13>`

```

In article <bWPGf.267023$OU5.121834@clgrps13>, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> writes:
> "Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
> news:dsg26602r6f@news3.newsguy.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> behaviour of having two of their arguments summed together.

Yes, but this is beside my original point, which is that saying "X
can be done by specialization" isn't interesting - anything can be
done by specialization.  Nothing about specialization guarantees that
new or overridden behaviors are relevant to the original design.

Thus when you wrote "I think this can be done by specialization", in
reference to my original example, you were trivially correct, but
it's not a useful claim.  The question (in that case, and in general)
would be whether specialization is the best way to make the necessary
change.  Often it is (and often when it isn't, that means it's time
for a new component), but my example was an argument that there are
cases where a change in requirements should affect existing users of
the components, because it applies to them as well.

That is, their requirements changed retroactively; or to be more
formally precise, they have a general requirement ("implement the
rules of DST") that remains in effect, but a derived specific
requirement ("DST requires we change the hour at times T1 and T2")
which has now been superceded.

In such a case, the old behavior is no longer applicable for any user
of the component, so the primary justification for specialization
doesn't apply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ DST in COBOL Programs (was Re: GoTo in Java)

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2006-02-10T11:25:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11upfpg43vurhfb@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com> <u5MFf.181020$km.67269@edtnps89> <dsakii01g7u@news1.newsguy.com> <HqoGf.184798$AP5.144580@edtnps84> <dsg26602r6f@news3.newsguy.com> <bWPGf.267023$OU5.121834@clgrps13> <dsi68u0283g@news1.newsguy.com>`

```

"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message
news:dsi68u0283g@news1.newsguy.com...
[snip]
> That is, their requirements changed retroactively; or to be more
> formally precise, they have a general requirement ("implement the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't apply.

One of the features of FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE
is the difference between local time and UTC found in
the last five characters. Because UTC is a fixed reference,
any changes in local time that result from DST must be
reflected by an adjustment in the last five characters of
CURRENT-DATE. Thus, if CURRENT-DATE is fully
supported by the COBOL runtime and DST is supported
by the OS, COBOL programs could be made immune
from changes in the transition dates for DST. Or, so it
would seem.
```

As usual, an excellent response, Michael.

I understand your position, but it hasn't changed mine :-).

I've made a few  quick responses below, but I really don't have time to 
labour this further.

I believe in components. I don't believe in maintaining them. I don't write 
large graphics applications or gaming or simulation software; my comments 
should be interpreted purely in the context of commercial business 
processes; accounting, administration, MIS, etc.


"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message 
news:ds7pnl0gc7@news4.newsguy.com...
>
> In article <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's fine in theory, and no doubt often achievable in practice.

I certainly is for me.

> However, there are simply going to be cases where a component can't
> be decomposed into definitely-bug-free pieces, and cases where it
> has to change because of a change in requirements that can't be
> captured by specialization (at least not in a sensible fashion).
>
As this point I was waiting for you your examples and was pleased to see you 
provided some... :-)

> For an example of the former, consider a GUI component that renders
> an image map.  Image rendering is notorious for security holes; we've
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> usefully decomposed into trivial components.

Having never written this kind fo software, I'll take your word for it. 
However, I believe if it was approached with the idea of simplification, 
encapsulation, and decomposition it has to be possible to make it better. 
Even in the most complex of engines there are sub-assemblies. I see it this 
way; I understand that you don't. I don't think you are wrong, but you have 
failed to persuade me it can't be done. :-)

  There's no reasonable
> option other than to put the (necessarily complex) processing of the
> image data format into a component and maintain it.
>

My experience has taught me that when people say "There's no alternative" or 
"It's the only way..." what they really mean is that they WANT to do it this 
way, OR they are unable to think of another way.  I've seen this countless 
times and there are invariably alternatives that weren't considered, because 
the people were too close to the problem, no discussion and brainstorming 
was done, or there was an entrenched culture of "That's the way we do things 
here" or similar.

I have adopted a Japanese proverb as the motto for my current project where 
it has been necessary to stress that several teams of people are involved 
and it is essential that the whole thing melds as one team. The proverb is: 
"None of us is as smart as all of us." I firmly believe in discussion and 
brainstorming, even on deeply technical problems. There is no room for 
technical prima donnas (or even the Project Manager :-)) to impose a 
solution without exploring all options available. If the guidelines are: 
"Simplify, encapsulate, decompose" somebody somewhere will build a less 
complex, more efficient, 'complex engine'. Just on that, I was interested to 
note in passing today in the NZ Herald technology pages, that a company here 
is building electric motors that are highly innovative. 
(http://www.nzherald.co.nz/search/story.cfm?storyid=00077DCE-ECE1-13E6-A35183027AF10240). 
I couldn't help thinking that it was a brave man who would decide to improve 
on the simple technology of an electric motor. Yet someone did. Success has 
been 10 years coming to Wellington Drive, but they have persevered and it is 
now paying off.


> For the latter, consider a calendar component which has rules for
> daylight savings time.  Either those rules can come from processing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> are localization issues.
Well, that's only 2 options... why stop there? What about public properties 
that allow DST to be off or on and specify when it is to start and stop? 
Anyone using the engine could specify at the time  they invoke it, what 
their DST requirements were. These can be parameters or properties and can 
be passed from anywhere you like, including a command line or control 
script. It would be tempting to include the location, but I wouldn't do 
that. Simply build a 'smarter calendar'. Then wrap the location data into 
another component that invokes the smarter calendar... The smarter calendar 
would not require maintenance (unless the rotation of the Earth was to 
change :-)) Location data would be passed through the 'location handling' 
component which would also be suitably parameterised. And so on...

Personally, in the interests of standardising and stabilising interfaces, I 
don't pass parameters to components; I use public properties instead. Set 
the properties to obtain the behaviour you want, invoke the component, get 
the results from the properties it set up when you ran its methods. Stable, 
simple, maintenance free.


 Moreover, you have situations like what
> we're faced with in the US right now, with a pending change to the
> rules for all the regions which observe DST, plus an incipient switch
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I disagree. If you build the components right they can cope with all of the 
above scenarios.

>> >> The rule of source code is over. The future belongs to object code.
>> >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hmmm... I don't plan on writing any critical business applications in 
VBScript, Turing-complete or not...

Just because something CAN be done, doesn't mean you necessarily want to do 
it.

I once wrote (for a bet) a computer program that read punched cards, 
processed the data on them, and produced printed output, using only the 
logical operators AND, OR, NOT, and XOR (plus branch, load and store 
registers - and I could have done without those by cheating and building 
them in memory form the BOOLEAN instructions...). That was Turing-complete 
(it looped through the input), but I wouldn't recommend people to adopt this 
form of application development :-)

>> But I think you would agree that  the script required to glue a bunch of
>> components into an application has nothing like the volume, verbosity or

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

I agree with that, but OO was a major step forward and components are

>> I don't agree about it being oversold; if anything, it is undersold. Just 
>> as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And there's also surprising resistance to change, and conservatism from 
people who you would expect to know better. They resist having programming 
standards imposed, that may require changes in how they write programs, 
never mind new languages, and, God Forbid! new TECHNIQUES and CONCEPTS!

>> From the day I first got remote procedure calls working in a DCOM+
>> environment that 'everyone knew' was unsafe and unstable and vulnerabe to

>
So has everything else. And DCOM+ today is NOT the DCOM+ of 6 years ago. I'm

the naysayers would have you believe, either...

>> (No-one has ever successfully hacked it. There are safeguards in place, 
>> both
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> been "hacked" right up until the point when they first were).

And there is no such thing as a bug free program, there are only programs 
that haven't crashed yet... What is the point of all this? We live in an 
imperfect world. We have to make compromises every day. I'll settle for 
something that runs NOW and delivers ROI, rather than wait ten years to make 
it impossible to penetrate (if that can EVER be truly achieved). Any system 
that is impenetrable is useless. There has to be legitimate access and then, 
by definition the system is not impenetrable.
>
> However, DCOM+ is beside the point.

I agree. I mentioned it purely in the interest of historical fact. My first 
great thrill in the RPC/Component world just happened to be in that 
environment. It was interesting that all the people who had predicted dire 
consequences and the likelihood that it would NEVER work, were surprisingly 
quiet when it ran for months without problem. After a year, they didn't like 
to be reminded of their gloomy prognostications, but by then they were 
writing COM components too...:-)

The quality of a particular

Pete Dashwood wrote:
<snip>

 years, but I wouldn't go back to it now...)
> 
>  >> Maintaining source code is yesterday's technology.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Although you did NOT say so, your argument could be extended a bit to
proclaim "the end of programming".   The components that would be needed
to accomplish any task, while naturally very numerous, would be nibbled
away at until very nearly all of them were written - like maybe 99% with
some specialists around to look after the few remaining ones.  Because,
as you said, it's the interface that counts, not what's under the hood:
as long as the object code of the components is compatible it dosn't
matter what language they're written in - indeed nor does it matter what
language calls them.

But this troubles me for a couple of reasons.  One is that knowledge of
the availability of components and a precise description of their
input/output/specifications/ would be necessary for their widespread
adoption to replace the corresponding re-inventing the wheel:
communications!  Maybe we'd need a "component foundation" something like
the standards groups we have now, but handling real artifacts rather
than metalanguages. But beyond that, I can't see that that the need for
new functions will ever cease to exist.  A new theory of something or
other with commercial applications would certainly need a new vocabulary
and implementations.

But - a question.  I don't fully understand how you'd cope with
un-spec'ed examples.  Suppose you have a component that calculates
payroll & deductions.  After 10 years there comes the case of someone
with 9 children under the age of 10: which the component
mis-calculates.  I think I know what you'd do to handle this: but
perhaps you'd explain?  I ask to be sure I have it right!

Pl

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-02-08T01:07:49+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44rgt7F3jcolU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <43E7964F.BF81BD92@mts.net>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:43E7964F.BF81BD92@mts.net...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> <snip>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> payroll & deductions.

I wouldn't, Peter. Such functionality would be built from a number of 
components.


After 10 years there comes the case of someone
> with 9 children under the age of 10: which the component
> mis-calculates.  I think I know what you'd do to handle this: but
> perhaps you'd explain?  I ask to be sure I have it right!
>

Why would the component miscalculate this? It would have been written to 
calculate all possibilities of children for all ages and years, up to say 30 
children in 29 years. The number of children and the number of years would 
be properties. Someone (Mia Farrow?) might adopt 14 children in 4 years; 
they are still valid deductions. (note that the validations of children and 
years would not be done in the deductions component; it would simply have 
its properties set with data validated by a component in the presentation 
layer, which would have applied the rules before invoking the deductions 
component.

Perhaps it is not a good example (?)

I'll address your comment regarding unspec'd requirement.

First off, the method I use to develop components does not require a spec 
anyway and you could argue that ALL (my)components are  "unspec'd".  (They 
'evolve' out of Use Cases... it's a RAD thing...) But I digress... Once the 
component is complete it has a Help file prepared that documents its 
Methods, Properties, and Events. That is the 'spec'. It does what it says it 
does.

Now let's say we need to extend what it does. Traditionally, we'd simply 
amend an existing Method or add another one. If it needs more data elements, 
we'd add a Property to it and make it public. I don't do that. Instead I 
write a new component that has the extra property (ies) and/or methods and 
use it to wrap the existing one. I now have all of the previous 
functionality, plus the new functionality under the one 'umbrella'. If 
another system or part of thie same system wants only the original 
functionality, no problem. That component still exists and still does what 
it has always done. There isn't even any need to regression test because all 
of this is encapsulated. Only the new functionality needs testing. The 
original component was written once and never maintained. The new one will 
be exactly the same. The new component has the new Methods and Properties, 
and can also access the old ones where needed. OO languages provide 
facilities to EXTEND classes (and inherit and override all their existing 
methods and properties), and there is also the facility to simply implement 
an interface to the old component, with the interface containing it's own 
methods or properties. Or, you can simply write a new wrapper component and 
let it invoke the old one if and when it needs to.

I know it is hard to imagine this but I really don't have time to explain in 
detail, and I avoid posting code here.

I'm not trying to get everyone to embrace OO COBOL (it is probably too late 
for that) and I really don't care whether people use component based 
approaches or not. I'm reporting simply what works for me.

HTH,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2006-02-10T09:40:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<43ECB3EC.69DFB5D9@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <S9SBf.194761$OU5.25957@clgrps13> <43qn0fF1oqu69U1@individual.net> <F77Cf.110590$AP5.18493@edtnps84> <1138298486.064822.222920@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <0lsCf.112291$m05.1772@clgrps12> <1138390683.396793.322920@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <43vp1uF1pnsa5U1@individual.net> <droi6h02jb4@news1.newsguy.com> <44bngcF1d24oU1@individual.net> <43E7964F.BF81BD92@mts.net> <44rgt7F3jcolU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
> >
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Perhaps it is, perhaps it isn't.  You could say that AFTER you'd
answered!  That way you would seem less dismissive.

Let me be more general, then.  Suppose a component starts giving
demonstrably incorrect results, several years after it's been put into
production.  Are you saying that you'd still use it but wrap it in some
other thing that corrects the error?  (I don't have the vocabulary down
pat here so I'll state it crudely).  This would apply in both cases:
where the original component is incorrect and where the requirements
have changed.  If I understand you correctly, then I can see a
possibility of an original component wrapped in several successive
layers of error correction and/or enhancement.  If so, I'm not sure that
you've gained anything.

I would agree that your moethid of development is "un-spec'ed".  But
that's because I regard specs as something developed BEFORE any coding
is done - a blueprint, if you like.  This would detail what is needed
without saying how it's to be done.  In fact, I don't see how it's
possible to write a program without specs - even if they consist only of
a thorough understanding of the problem.  (Spec: I must get to Toronto
next week, by Thursday).  What you call a spec I'd call documentation,
because it's post-facto.  It is extremely important that such a thing
exists for the benefit of others and yourself ten years from now at
2a.m. in an emergency, whatever we might call it.  (Documentation:
WestJet flight 666, YWG - YZZ, 8:55 a.m., Thursday).  So I
wholeheartedly approve of that (as if you need my approval!).

A further question: are "your" components used only by yourself?

PL
```

```
In article <43ECB3EC.69DFB5D9@mts.net>, Peter Lacey  <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> children in 29 years.

[snip]

>> Perhaps it is not a good example (?)
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>production.

A step back might be in order.  Perhaps the example given was not the 
best; consider, then, one of a component that calculates tax-related sums.

Tax laws change.  The component is still giving results which are correct 
for the conditions/laws for which it was written but the laws have since 
changed... for example, the amount of income which can be deducted for a 
child goes from US$750 to US$1000. 

>Are you saying that you'd still use it but wrap it in some
>other thing that corrects the error?

Or, in the words of an American Express commercial several decades old... 
'What *will* you do?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-02-11T16:01:05+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4552brF50galU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <43E7964F.BF81BD92@mts.net> <44rgt7F3jcolU1@individual.net> <43ECB3EC.69DFB5D9@mts.net> <dsiheg$36a$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dsiheg$36a$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <43ECB3EC.69DFB5D9@mts.net>, Peter Lacey  <lacey@mts.net> 
> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> child goes from US$750 to US$1000.

I would expect the interface to this component to accommodate such a change. 
In other words there would be a property or a parameter defined at the time 
the component was built which would allow a different deduction to be 
passed.

Some people would use:

invoke TaxCalcObj "childDeductions"
           USING  ws-deduction
           RETURNING payableTax
end-invoke

I don't do it that way. I don't want the interface to my component to change 
so I achieve this by the use of properties:

The property could be set at run time (as in the example below) when the 
component is invoked, or it could be set once in the factory at the start of 
the day...(Then every instantiation of the TaxCalcObject would have this 
value available to it)

invoke TaxCalcObj "SET-kidsDeductionAmt"
            USING ws-deduction
end-invoke
invoke TaxCalcObj "childDeductions"
           RETURNING payableTax
end-invoke

Doing it this way there is no change to the interface. Every time you want 
tax amount after child deductions you just invoke the component.

invoke TaxCalcObj "childDeductions"
           RETURNING payableTax
end-invoke

Defining what are invariant variables (parameters) and what are true 
variables is something that comes out of the object modelling and the Use 
Case. Any fields required for calculation that could be subject to change in 
the future, would be defined in factory and given default values.

If the whole basis for handling child deductions was changed in an 
unforeseeable way in the future, the component would be rewritten. The 
interface would remain exactly the same and everything would continue to 
work.

>
>>Are you saying that you'd still use it but wrap it in some
>>other thing that corrects the error?

No. Personally, I would never wrap or continue using a flawed component. It 
would be replaced, just like a faulty circuit board in your home movie 
system.
>
> Or, in the words of an American Express commercial several decades old...
> 'What *will* you do?'
>
I would leave home without it :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoTo in Java

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-02-12T01:28:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4563ioF54fd7U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<dnnqs1tjutcc64coojpi0k06g2jrlopf38@4ax.com> <43ECB3EC.69DFB5D9@mts.net> <dsiheg$36a$1@reader2.panix.com> <4552brF50galU1@individual.net> <dskfhd$ddh$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dskfhd$ddh$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <4552brF50galU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> at the code level and not at the user level.

Parameters (if passed) are passed to object code. Properties (if set) are 
set in object code. The factory object (if used) generates object code of 
object instances.  No source is necessary or even (in my opinion) desirable.
>
>>In other words there would be a property or a parameter defined at the 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> now.
I didn't realize we were discussing "how it gets done now". I thought I was 
explaining a component based approach. It may well be similar to how things 
"get done now" but there are subtle (yet very important) differences.One of 
them is that source code doesn't need to be maintained. There are too many 
others to go into here.


> What happens when the calculation changes and a different formula
> gets used?

Then a new component is required. (I thought I said that...) People who were 
in the business of selling tax systems would probably provide the new 
component automatically. The interface remains the same. Major changes in 
system functionality may require new methods.  If both new properties and 
new methods were required they would be provided through a new component or 
by wrapping the old one and overriding the existing method. No attempt is 
ever made to maintain the old component, so no source code is required. It 
does what it does.

I should add that there are many sites and people who DON'T use components 
like this and DO maintain the existing components.  My experience has been 
that these are sites used to traditional programming and approaches.

I don't use those approaches and I don't maintain components. (I am not the 
only one...:-))
>
Read it again, Doc. I covered all of this in the last post. I also explained 
why a parameter DOESN'T get passed, although it could be done that way.

Pete.
```
